coredata.dc:title: Retrieve of residual waste of carbon dots derived from straw mushroom as a hydrochar for the removal of organic dyes from aqueous solutions
coredata.dc:creator: Zulfajri, Muhammad
Kao, Yu-Tung
Huang, Genin Gary
coredata.dc:description:
The residual waste during the separation of carbon dots (C-dots) solution derived from natural resources was usually discarded without further use. In this study, the simultaneous production of C-dots and hydrochar (HC) from straw mushroom through hydrothermal carbonization treatment was carried out to increase the utilities of sustainable resources. The obtained C-dots exhibited good physicochemical and optical properties. The residual waste was retrieved as the HC for the removal of organic dyes. The HC had the BET surface of 11.68 m2/g with an average pore size and pore volume of 15.42 nm and 0.045 m3/g, respectively. The HC showed excellent removal efficiencies for crystal violet (CV) and methylene blue (MB) from aqueous solutions of more than 90%. Under Langmuir's isotherm, the maximum adsorption capacity (q max) of the HC was 1.88 mg/g and 3.11 mg/g for MB and CV, respectively. The adsorption capacities at time (q
t ) of MB and CV were in the range of 1.85–1.94 mg/g and 2.46–2.65 mg/g, respectively. The equilibrium stage happened above 30 min with a mixing time of 2 min. The HC could completely adsorb 2.5 × 1016 molecules of 25 μM MB and 3.0 × 1016 molecules of 50 μM CV. The original colors of MB and CV were almost completely removed after introducing the HC to the dye solutions, which demonstrates the possibility of overcoming the environmental pollution problems by sustainably residual waste materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrochar
Organic dyes removal
Sustainable material

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Retrieve of HC during the preparation of C-dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Taiwan Ministry of Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Taiwan Ministry of Science and Technology ( MOST107-2113-M-037-013 ) and the Kaohsiung Medical University ( KMU-M110008 and KMUH-DK(B)110001-4 ). The authors would also like to thank Prof. Chien-Hung Li, Prof. Chai-Lin Kao, and Prof. Sodio C.N. Hsu for their fruitful discussion on the work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The chemical structures of selected organic dyes.
The simultaneous preparation of C-dots and HC and the utilization of HC for removal of MB and CV dyes.
(a) Raman, (b) FTIR, and (c) UV–Vis spectra of the obtained HC.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of (a) MO, (b) EB, (c) RhB, (d) RB, (e) R6G, (f) MB, and (g) CV before (colored lines) and after introducing HC to the dye solutions (black line). (h) The adsorption efficiency percentages of HC for different organic dyes. Conditions: 100 μM of dyes' concentration, 15 mg of HC dosage, 2 min of mixing time, and 60 min of incubation time.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of (a) MO and MO + HC, (b) EB and EB + HC, and (c) the enhancement of HC's adsorption percentages for MO and EB from 100 to 50 μM. Conditions: 50 μM and 100 μM of dye concentrations and 15 mg of HC dosage.
(a) The A/A0 at ~660 nm of MB + HC and (b) A/A0 at ~580 nm of CV + HC at different mixing times (1, 2, 4, and 8 min). Conditions: 100 μM of dyes, 15 mg of HC dosage, and 60 min of incubation time.

(a) The A/A0 at ~660 nm of MB + HC and (b) A/A0 at ~580 nm of CV + HC at different incubation times (5, 10, 15, 30, 60, 120, and 180 min). Conditions: 100 μM of dyes, 15 mg of HC dosage, and 2 min of mixing time.

The comparison of adsorption efficiency percentages of HC for (a) MB and (b) CV at different concentrations (25, 50, 75, and 100 μM). Experimental conditions: 15 mg of HC dosage, 2 min of mixing time, and 60 min of incubation time.
Possible adsorption mechanisms of representative dyes on the HC.
BET surface area, pore volume, and pore diameter of hydrochars obtained from different starting raw materials.
Isotherm parameters for removal of MB and CV by HC.
The adsorption capacity of obtained HC compared to other HCs.
Kinetic parameters for removal of MB and CV by HC.

head.ce:title: Retrieve of residual waste of carbon dots derived from straw mushroom as a hydrochar for the removal of organic dyes from aqueous solutions

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Medicinal and Applied Chemistry, Kaohsiung Medical University, Kaohsiung, 80708, Taiwan.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Medicinal and Applied Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The residual waste during the separation of carbon dots (C-dots) solution derived from natural resources was usually discarded without further use. In this study, the simultaneous production of C-dots and hydrochar (HC) from straw mushroom through hydrothermal carbonization treatment was carried out to increase the utilities of sustainable resources. The obtained C-dots exhibited good physicochemical and optical properties. The residual waste was retrieved as the HC for the removal of organic dyes. The HC had the BET surface of 11.68 m2/g with an average pore size and pore volume of 15.42 nm and 0.045 m3/g, respectively. The HC showed excellent removal efficiencies for crystal violet (CV) and methylene blue (MB) from aqueous solutions of more than 90%. Under Langmuir's isotherm, the maximum adsorption capacity (q max) of the HC was 1.88 mg/g and 3.11 mg/g for MB and CV, respectively. The adsorption capacities at time (q
t ) of MB and CV were in the range of 1.85–1.94 mg/g and 2.46–2.65 mg/g, respectively. The equilibrium stage happened above 30 min with a mixing time of 2 min. The HC could completely adsorb 2.5 × 1016 molecules of 25 μM MB and 3.0 × 1016 molecules of 50 μM CV. The original colors of MB and CV were almost completely removed after introducing the HC to the dye solutions, which demonstrates the possibility of overcoming the environmental pollution problems by sustainably residual waste materials.

ce:section.ce:para: The fast development of urbanization and industrialization along with the enhancing discharge of the human population has carried dangerous water pollution, leading to the worldwide reason of diseases and deaths (Du et al., 2020). There are many chemical pollutants such as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon, heavy metals, dyes, pesticides, herbicides, phthalates, detergents, and many others (Akhtar and Mannan, 2020). The effluents of many industries using organic dyes in their activities are arising as an environmentally challenging problem worldwide because many dyes are toxic and stable to light and oxidants, which make them not easy to degrade (Li et al., 2013). Organic dyes are primary chemical compounds and are highly used in many industries, especially in the textile industry to colorize their products (Zhou et al., 2019). In this industry, wastewater is one of the most contaminant sources of water systems (Katheresan et al., 2018). Some organic dyes, including anionic dyes (methyl orange, eosin B, and rose Bengal) and cationic dyes (rhodamine B, rhodamine 6G, methylene blue, and crystal violet), were used. Their chemical structures are depicted in Fig. 1 . Until now, the catalysis and adsorption methods are the most effective ways to treat industrial wastewaters and have been highly utilized in research laboratories (Singh et al., 2018; Yahya et al., 2018).

Carbon-based materials have excellent properties such as high chemical stability, superior electrical conductivity, excellent physical and chemical properties, excellent ductility and porous structure, and high surface area (Li et al., 2020). These materials are widely used as adsorbents (Moreno-Castilla and Rivera-Utrilla, 2001), catalyst supports (Lam and Luong, 2014), and energy storage devices (Iqbal et al., 2019). Meanwhile, nature serves abundant natural resources for the preparation of new carbon materials with unique properties. For example, the preparation of carbon dots (C-dots) from natural resources is facile, cheap, and eco-friendly which prevents the utilization of hazardous and toxic materials and complicated reaction conditions (Das et al., 2018; Zulfajri et al., 2019b). However, the residual waste after the preparation of C-dots from natural resources is commonly discarded without further use. Therefore, it is important to utilize this residual waste as a valuable material for the sake of environmentally friendly purposes.

Hydrochar (HC) is a carbonaceous solid organic material as the main product of the hydrothermal carbonization process after conversion at a medium temperature from 180 to 350 °C in the existence of water under autogenous pressures for certain hours (Kantakanit et al., 2018; Qian et al., 2018). It is defined as a thermochemical process for converting organic compounds into carbon-rich materials (Libra et al., 2011). The adsorption ability of HC is largely utilized as an effective and reliable treatment for the removal of dye pollutants. Recently, some literatures reported the successful preparation of HC by hydrothermal carbonization treatment from available natural resources, such as bamboo (Qian et al., 2018), hickory and peanut hull (Fang et al., 2017), coconut shell (Islam et al., 2017a), rattan (Islam et al., 2017b), garlic peel (Huang et al., 2019), and Cattail leaves (Jaruwat et al., 2018). Although the use of natural resources is an effective way to prepare HC, the residual waste material from other synthetic products is challenging to utilize for HC with satisfactory physical, chemical, and optical properties.

In this work, the preparation of HC retrieved from the residual waste of C-dots derived from straw mushroom through a hydrothermal carbonization treatment was carried out to enhance the utilization efficiency of natural resources. The proposed method was facile, cheap, and eco-friendly in which the residual waste of C-dots as HC was produced. The obtained HC was characterized using Raman, FTIR, and UV–Vis spectroscopy. The HC as a promising adsorbent was then used for the removal of several organic dyes. The removal/adsorption performances of HC for different organic dyes were evaluated and discussed.

The straw mushroom was purchased from a 24 h grocery store in Kaohsiung City, Taiwan. Crystal Violet (CV), Rose Bengal (RB), and Eosin B (EB) were obtained from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA). Methyl Orange (MO) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Methylene Blue (MB), Rhodamine 6G (R6G), and Rhodamine B (RhB) were obtained from Acros Organics (Fair Lawn, NJ, USA). All obtained chemicals with analytical grade were used without any purification. All dyes were diluted with ultrapure water from SimplicityTM Water Purification System (Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Retrieve of HC during the preparation of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The C-dots derived from straw mushroom were produced by a hydrothermal treatment method as reported in our previous paper (Zulfajri et al., 2020). After cooling down the solution to ambient temperature, the solution of C-dots was filtered using a Filter Paper Grade No. 1 (Advantec MFS, Inc., Dublin, CA, USA) followed by centrifugation (Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA, USA). Both the residual wastes in the bottom of the Teflon autoclave (Tefic Biotech Co. Ltd.) and those on the filter paper were collected and washed several times with the excess amount of ultrapure water. Finally, the clean residual wastes were dried overnight at 80 °C in an oven and demolished into fine powder as the HC material.

The fluorescent and UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured using an Agilent Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Santa Clara, CA, USA) and a Shimadzu UV-1800 Spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan), respectively. The morphology and functional groups of samples were explored using a ProTrusTech-Micro Raman Identify Spectrometer (Tainan, Taiwan) and a Bruker-ALPHA FTIR Spectrometer (MA, USA). The heating process was carried out in a channel precision oven. The samples were mixed using a vortex mixer (Model VTX-3000L Mixer Uzusio-LMS, Tokyo, Japan). The samples were centrifuged using Eppendorf Centrifuge 5424 (Hamburg, Germany). The specific Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area, pore size, and pore volume were determined by a Micromeritics ASAP-2050 porosimeter (Norcross, GA, USA).

The HC was used as an adsorbent material after crushing the dried residual wastes without further treatment. Typically, 15 mg of HC powder was added separately into dye solutions (100 μM, 1000 μL). The mixtures were stirred for 2 min followed by incubation for 1 h. Then, the centrifugation of the mixture solutions was performed at 5000 rpm for 10 min to precipitate the adsorbent from the solutions. The upper layer solutions containing dyes were collected by pipette and the solutions were again centrifuged for 5 min to completely remove the remaining adsorbent. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of dye solutions before and after adding HC were monitored to ascertain the adsorption capacity. To optimize the adsorption conditions, different mixing times, incubation times, dye concentrations, and HC dosages were examined.

The adsorption efficiency percentage and adsorption capacity were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2) below:
(1)
A d s o r p t i o n
( % )
=
C i

C e
C i
x
100
(2)
q =
(
C i
−
C e
)
V
W
where q is the adsorption capacity, C
i and C
e (mg/L) are initial and equilibrium dye concentrations, respectively. V (L) is the dye solution volume, and W (g) is the HC dosage.

Adsorption isotherms including Langmuir, Freundlich, and Temkin model were applied to explain the equilibrium adsorption characteristics. The Langmuir's isotherm in its linear form and the separation factor were calculated using Eqs. (3) and (4) below:
(3)

=
1

+
1
q max
(4)

=
1
1 +
C i

K L
where qe (mg/g) is the adsorption capacity, KL (L/mg) is the Langmuir's isotherm constant, qmax (mg/g) is the maximum adsorption capacity, and RL is the separation factor. RL is the dimensionless Langmuir constant which indicates the adsorption possibility either favorable (0 < RL < 1), unfavorable (RL > 1), linear (RL = 1), or irreversible (RL = 0).

The Freundlich's isotherm in linear form was represented Eq. (5) below:
(5)
L o g
q e
= L o g
K F
+
1 n
L o g
C e
where KF is the Freundlich's constant, 1/n is the adsorption intensity. The adsorption process is favorable (0.1 < 1/n < 0.5) or unfavorable (1/n > 2).

The Temkin's isotherm is shown in Eq. (6) below:
(6)
q e
=
R T
B T
ln
K T
+
R T
B T
ln
C e
where RT/BT or B (kcal/mol) is the heat of adsorption, R is the gas constant, T is the absolute temperature in K, BT (J/mol) is the adsorption energy variation, and KT (L/mol) is the equilibrium binding constant corresponding to the maximum binding energy.

Adsorption rates were analyzed using Pseudo-first-order and Pseudo-second-order kinetic models. The Pseudo-first-order and Pseudo-second-order kinetic models are represented in Eqs. (7) and (8) below, respectively.
(7)
ln
(
q e
−
q t
)
= ln
q e
−
K 1
t
(8)
t
q e
=
1
K 2
q e 2
+
1
q e
where, q
t (mg/g) is the adsorption capacity at time t, K
1 (min−1) is the equilibrium rate constant for the first order, K
2 (g/mg.min) is the equilibrium rate constant for the second order. The linear coefficient regression (R2) values are used to predict the most suited isotherm and kinetic model for the adsorption process.

In this study, a hydrothermal carbonization treatment to simultaneously obtain C-dots and HC from straw mushroom as a precursor was performed as illustrated in Fig. 2 . The straw mushroom powder was transformed into microsphere cores through dehydration and polymerization in the hydrothermal autoclave at 200 °C for 25 h. The dispersed colloidal particles were then formed from the microsphere cores by the adsorption of surrounding molecules and lastly developed into HC material (Guo et al., 2017). A burst in the nucleation of C-dots happened when the concentration of aromatic groups of polymers achieves the supersaturated point in which the peptide chains were then carbonized with increasing the particle numbers of C-dots through continuous reaction time and further the polymeric fragment disappeared (Zulfajri et al., 2019a). The separation of the solution phase from the upper solution and bottom precipitate was carried out using centrifugation. The upper solution exhibited fluorescence character when irradiated with UV-light, indicating the formation of C-dots. The C-dots were stable, yellowish-brown, and clear solution without precipitation. The HC was obtained from residual waste after washing several times with excess water and dried in an oven to obtain a brownish-black powder. Finally, the HC was utilized as an adsorbent for the removal of organic dyes from aqueous solutions.

The C-dots have spherical shapes with a narrow size distribution in the range of 3–8 nm (Zulfajri et al., 2020). The C-dots also owned several functional groups including the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H (3351-3095 cm−1), C–H (2951 cm−1), CO (1665 cm−1), CC (1585 cm−1), –COOH/C–N (1410 cm−1), and C–O (1073 cm−1) bonds (Fig. S1a). Some other bands in the lower wavenumbers also proved the presence of C–O, N–H, O–H, and C–H bonds which support the excellent water solubility of C-dots. Then, the C-dots showed the unique excitation-dependent emission behavior with the red-shift character. The optimum emission intensity of C-dots was observed at 440 nm excited at 360 nm (Fig. S1b). The C-dots possessed cyan color under UV-light irradiation as shown inset of Fig. S1b. In addition, the UV–Vis spectrum of C-dots exhibited a broad absorption band from 250 to 600 nm (Fig. S1c). The absorbances at about 270 nm and 330 nm come from π-π* energy transition of CC bonds and n-π* energy transition of CC bonds, respectively (Ansi and Renuka, 2018). The data confirm that the C-dots have good optical characters.

The Raman spectrum of the prepared HC showed typical carbon materials with D and G bands (Fig. 3 a) (Sheng, 2007). The D band and G band were observed at ~1345 cm-1 and ~1592 cm-1, respectively. The vibration mode of the D band including the peak intensity and the width of the peak was related to a disordered graphitic lattice (Fuertes et al., 2010). Meanwhile, the G band was related to a good graphitic lattice. Therefore, the D mode is used as an indicator of the crystalline order degree in graphene-like materials. The peak intensity ratio (ID /IG ) value was 0.42, indicating that the HC has a more graphitic structure with enough structural defects.

The typical FTIR spectrum of the HC is shown in Fig. 3b. The HC has several bands observed at 3186 cm-1 (O–H bonds), 2903 cm-1 (C–H bonds), 1585 cm-1 (CO/CC bonds), 1432 cm-1 (-COOH bonds), and 1287 cm−1 (C–O–C bonds). Several other bands observed at lower wavenumbers also demonstrated the existence of various bonds such as C–H, O–H, N–H, and C–O. The obtained data shows that the HC contained oxygen-functional groups that derived during the carbonization process of the precursor. Fig. 3c depicts the UV–Vis spectrum of the HC solution. UV–Vis spectrum was measured by mixing 10 mg of HC with 1 mL ultrapure water and sonicated the mixture for 10 min to break up the HC particles into smaller fractions. The absorbance peaks of CC with π-π* energy transitions and CO bonds with n-π* energy transitions were observed at ~270 nm and ~330 nm, respectively. The UV–Vis spectrum proved the existence of oxygen-containing groups that attached to the carbon skeleton in the HC surface.

Furthermore, the textural characters of HC such as BET surface area, pore size, and pore volume were obtained by analysis of N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms measured at 77 K. The BET surface area of HC was calculated as 11.68 m2/g with an average pore size of 15.42 nm and pore volume of 0.045 m3/g. Based on the surface area and pore size results, the introduction of HC favors the enhancement of the adsorption capacity of HC due to more surface sites for the adsorption of dye molecules. Besides, it is competitive when it comes to the cost-effective method for the preparation of HC compared to other adsorbents with complicated and non-green procedures. Table 1 shows the hydrochar's textural characteristics which were directly prepared from hydrothermal carbonization treatment under different conditions and with several precursors.

HC plays a prominent role as a multipurpose adsorbent material. HC is usually used in different fields such as in wastewater purification, water retention, soil amendment, soil fertility, energy storage, solid fuel, as well as the removal of heavy metals, phosphates, organic pollutants, and pathogens (Azzaz et al., 2020; Fang et al., 2018). In this work, HC was derived as a beneficial and low-cost functional material during the synthesis of C-dots. The HC was performed as an adsorbent for the removal of several organic dyes including MO, EB, RhB, RB, R6G, MB, and CV. Fig. 4 a–g shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of 100 μM dyes and the mixture of the dyes with 15 mg HC. The absorption bands of the dyes were monitored from 300 to 850 nm. When introducing the HC into different dye solutions, the absorbance peaks were decreased with different percentages followed by fading the original dye colors. The HC could adsorb all organic dyes with different adsorption capacity percentages.

Based on their reduction in the solution color and absorbance peak intensity, the HC possessed the highest adsorption capacity for MB and CV due to their highest binding affinity to HC compared to other dyes (Fig. 4h). On the other hand, the binding affinities of MO and EB to HC were less than other dyes. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of 50 μM MO and EB and their mixture with 15 mg HC were presented in Fig. 5 a and Fig. 5b, respectively. The adsorption capacities of HC for MO and EB were increased with decreasing the concentration of both dyes from 100 μM to 50 μM (Fig. 5c). Hence, the HC showed the adsorption potential for almost representative organic dyes from the aqueous solutions.

To further explore the adsorption capacity of the obtained HC, different HC dosages were added into 100 μM MB and CV solutions. Based on the results, the HC with different dosages exhibited different adsorption capacity for MB and CV dyes. As shown in Fig. S2a, the absorbance peak of MB at ~660 nm was reduced by increasing the HC dosages from 0 to 20 mg with ~93% adsorption efficiency. The absorbance peak of CV at ~580 nm was also reduced by increasing the HC dosages from 0 to 20 mg with an adsorption efficiency of ~96.7% (Fig. S2b). Moreover, Fig. S3 exhibits the appearance of CV and MB solutions with HC at different dosages (1= 5 mg, 2= 10 mg, 3= 15 mg, 4= 20 mg, 5= without HC) after incubation for 1 h (black box), centrifugation for 10 min (red box), and re-centrifugation for 5 min and removing the HC from the dye solutions (green box). The MB blue color and CV purple color were almost completely disappeared after adding HC at different dosages into MB (Fig. S3a) and CV (Fig. S3b) solutions.

Furthermore, various mixing times from 1 to 8 min using vortex mixer slightly affected the reduction of absorbance peaks of MB (Fig. 6 a) and CV (Fig. 6b). Based on the observation, 2 min was ideal for mixing the dye/HC mixture before incubating them at room temperature. Besides, the incubation times were also evaluated to obtain the adequate time for the optimum adsorption capacity of HC for MB and CV. Several incubation times were carried out after mixing the dyes with HC for 2 min. It can be seen that the incubation times from 5 to 180 min of the mixtures slightly perturbed the absorbance peaks of MB (Fig. 7 a) and CV (Fig. 7b). 60 min was selected as an ideally moderate incubation time of the mixtures for the optimum adsorption capacity with an equilibrium stage. The adsorption capacities at time (qt ) for 1 mL MB (32 mg/L) and CV (41 mg/L) solutions with 15 mg HC were about 1.85–1.94 mg/g (27.81–29.06 mg/L) and 2.46–2.65 mg/g (36.90–39.73 mg/L), respectively. From all findings, it is suggested that 15 mg of HC dosage, 2 min of mixing time, and 60 min of incubation time were used as the optimum conditions for the removal of 100 μM MB and CV dyes in 1 mL aqueous solutions.

Besides, 15 mg HC with different MB and CV concentrations was also evaluated to assess the specific dye concentration that can be adsorbed completely by the HC. The comparison of MB and CV's absorbance behavior at different concentrations from 25 to 100 μM was depicted in Figs. S4a–b. The absorbance behavior was proportional to the concentration of the dyes. Subsequently, the data were used to compare the removal capacity of HC for different concentrations of MB and CV according to their reduction in absorbance. Figs. S5a–d show the absorbance peaks of different MB concentrations (25, 50, 75, and 100 μM) and after the introduction of 15 mg HC. The removal of MB from the aqueous solution can be seen Inset of Figs. S5a–d, where the blue color was completely disappeared especially for 25 μM MB. The same procedures were carried out to remove CV from the aqueous solution. As shown in Figs. S6a–d, the absorbance peaks of CV with different concentrations (25–100 μM) were reduced by introducing the HC. The purple color was disappeared after adding the HC into the CV solution, indicating the successful removal of the CV (Inset of Figs. S6a–d).

Fig. 8 a shows the adsorption efficiency percentages of HC for different MB concentrations. The HC almost completely adsorbed 25 μM MB with an adsorption efficiency of ~98.9%, meaning that the HC could adsorb 2.5 × 10-8 mol or 1.5 × 1016 molecules of 25 μM MB in the aqueous solution. Meanwhile, the adsorption efficiency percentages of CV at different concentrations are shown in Fig. 8b. The HC almost adsorbed completely 50 μM CV with ~98.8% adsorption efficiency. HC could adsorb 5.0 × 10−8 mol or 3.0 × 1016 molecules of 50 μM CV in the aqueous solution. Hence, it can be deduced that 15 mg HC can completely remove 25 μM MB and 50 μM CV at optimum conditions.

The initial concentration of MB and CV affected the adsorption capacity of the HC. An increasing trend in the adsorption capacity of HC for MB and CV was observed (Fig. 9 ). HC exhibited excellent adsorption capacity due to its high affinity toward MB and CV. An adsorption isotherm provides useful information about the adsorption capacity, binding affinity, and surface properties of HC which help to understand their binding mechanism. In this study, three isotherm models were used to examine the adsorption behavior of HC for MB and CV (Fig. S7). The Langmuir's isotherm describes the monolayer/homogeneous adsorption of the dyes onto the HC surface having a finite number of adsorption sites, which implies that no further adsorption can occur at the adsorption site after a dye molecule occupies the site. Freundlich's isotherm supports that the adsorption occurs on the heterogeneous HC surface (Sierra-Trejo et al., 2020). Temkin's isotherm model is chosen to evaluate the adsorption potentials of the HC for the dyes.

The adsorption isotherm parameters of the Langmuir, Freundlich, and Temkin models were summarized in Table 2 . Both Langmuir and Temkin isotherm models had a good correlation with the adsorption process. Langmuir and Temkin isotherm models with R
2 of above 0.99 were the best fitted for adsorption of MB and CV. Their R
2 values were higher than that of the Freundlich isotherm model (>0.95). Therefore, the Freundlich isotherm model was not suitable to describe the adsorption process. The finding verifies the monolayer/homogeneous adsorption of MB and CV onto the HC surface and strong interaction between the dyes and HC active groups (Wathukarage et al., 2017). The R
L values for HC were less than 1 and the 1/n values were above 0.1 and below 0.5, confirming the favorable of the adsorption process phenomenon (Wathukarage et al., 2017). The maximum adsorption capacities were calculated to be 1.88 mg/g and 3.11 mg/g for MB and CV, respectively. Table 3 presents the q
max of other HCs for various pollutants from previous studies and the present study. The obtained HC had a desirable, high affinity for dye molecules and the maximum adsorption capacity was comparable for some other HCs.

For Temkin's isotherm model, the relatively high K
T values for the adsorption indicate a high potential of HC for the adsorption of MB and CV (Sun et al., 2015). This might be attributed to the high electrostatic interaction between dyes and HC (Jnr and Spiff, 2005). The positive B
T values indicated that the adsorption process was exothermic. The B values were calculated to be 1.496 kcal/mol and 0.874 ≈ 1 kcal/mol for MB and CV, respectively. The physisorption and chemisorption occur when B is less than 1.0 kcal/mol and between 20 and 50 kcal/mol, respectively (Kim et al., 2006). But, both mechanisms occur if the B value is 1.0–20 kcal/mol. Therefore, it is believed that both physisorption and chemisorption could be involved in the adsorption processes of MB and CV.

The adsorption kinetic curves for MB and CV onto the HC were shown in Fig. 10 . The adsorption process of HC for MB and CV rapidly attained a high adsorption and the equilibrium was achieved above 30 min with rapid and optimum adsorption after 5 min. This rapid kinetic behavior indicates that the adsorption process depended upon the available binding sites on the HC for the adsorption of MB and CV molecules. The HC surface becomes saturated with the dyes after 60 min of incubation, and finally, the dynamic equilibrium was achieved. Therefore, this method exhibited a rapid and effective adsorption process for the removal of organic dyes.

In this study, the Pseudo-first-order and Pseudo-second-order kinetic models were employed to evaluate the kinetic parameters of the adsorption of MB and CV onto the HC. The plots of two adsorption kinetic models to MB and CV equilibrium data are depicted in Fig. S8, and the respective kinetic parameters were represented in Table 4 . The Pseudo-first-order kinetic model could not be used to describe the adsorption kinetics because the R
2 of the Pseudo-first-order kinetic model for both MB (R2 = 0.62520) and CV (R2 = 0.79751) were not close to 1, graph line was not passing through all the dots, and the qe,exp and qe,cal were not close to each other (Ayub et al., 2020). The Pseudo-second-order kinetic model was observed to be the best-fitted model to explain the adsorption kinetics of MB and CV on the HC. It showed higher R
2 values above 0.999 for both dyes. Their qe,exp and qe,cal values were almost similar which favors the Pseudo-second-order kinetic model. The findings reveal that the dye concentrations and incubation times were responsible for the rate-determining step during the dye-binding onto the HC. This also suggests that the adsorption of MB and CV onto HC takes place through a chemisorption mechanism (Ayub et al., 2020). These results suggest that the boundary layer resistance is not a rate-limiting factor because MB and CV adsorption process is following the Pseudo-second-order kinetic model (Sun et al., 2015).

The adsorption capacity of HC for MB and CV can be suggested to the physical and chemical adsorption mechanisms. For the physisorption mechanism, the dye molecules were directly adsorbed into the surface of HC material. For the chemisorption mechanism, the hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interaction, and π-π interaction between HC and dye molecules occurred when HC adsorbs the organic dyes (Mashkoor et al., 2018; Sahu et al., 2020). The electrostatic interaction happened between the positively charged nitrogen center of MB/CV and the negatively charged (-COO−) of HC. The hydrogen bonding happened between the nitrogen/sulfur of MB or nitrogen of CV and –OH groups of HC. The π-π interaction happened between the benzene rings of organic dyes and the aromatic backbone of HC. The possible interactions of MB and CV adsorptions by the HC material were illustrated in Fig. 11 .

In this study, a simple and green simultaneous synthesis of C-dots and HC derived from straw mushroom was performed. The C-dots demonstrated excellent optical properties and high water solubility. The HC was a low-cost and sustainable material that offers the use of residual waste of C-dots as a functional adsorbent material. The HC can easily and significantly remove the organic dyes, especially for MB and CV. It is revealed that the obtained HC was a functional material from residual material, promoting the eco-friendly and cheap adsorbent for effective removal of organic dye pollutants from the water systems to save the environment and human health. For future study, the obtained HC from straw mushroom can be prepared to be an activated HC to improve its adsorption capacity for various pollutants. Besides, the increasing of the incubation time with a low HC dosage and high solution volume could be also considered to explore its adsorption capacity.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Taiwan Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST107-2113-M-037-013) and the Kaohsiung Medical University (KMU-M110008 and KMUH-DK(B)110001-4). The authors would also like to thank Prof. Chien-Hung Li, Prof. Chai-Lin Kao, and Prof. Sodio C.N. Hsu for their fruitful discussion on the work.
